target.title: fix(ollama): release stream reader lock to prevent resource leak
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/ollama/src/stream.ts` acquires a `ReadableStreamDefaultReader` via `response.body.getReader()` inside a try block, but the finally block that calls `release()` did not call `reader.releaseLock()`. Per the Streams specification, `releaseLock()` is required to release the lock on the underlying stream — without it, the stream remains locked after the function returns.

Additionally, the original `const reader` declaration inside the try block caused a TypeScript error: the finally block cannot reference a const that is not guaranteed to be assigned in all code paths (error paths throw before reader assignment).

## Changes

- `extensions/ollama/src/stream.ts`: hoist `reader` declaration before try block → `let reader: ReadableStreamDefaultReader<Uint8Array> | undefined`
- Assign `reader` inside try: `reader = response.body.getReader()`
- In finally: `reader?.releaseLock()` (optional chaining for safety when try throws before assignment)

Label: bugfix | merge-risk: 🟢 minimal | AI-assisted

## Evidence

### Code path analysis

Before the fix:
```
try {
  reader = body.getReader()   // may throw before this line
  while(true) { read() }      // stream processing
} finally {
  // reader might be unassigned → TS error
  await release()
}
```

After the fix:
```
let reader: ... | undefined
try {
  reader = body.getReader()   // safe: declared outside
  while(true) { read() }
} finally {
  reader?.releaseLock()       // safe: optional chaining
  await release()
}
```

The `?.` operator ensures that if the try block throws before `reader` is assigned (e.g., non-OK response, empty body), the finally block degrades gracefully to a no-op for `releaseLock()` while still executing `await release()`.

### Reference

Per the Streams specification: "A ReadableStream reader lock is released by calling `reader.releaseLock()`. This must be called even after `reader.cancel()` — cancel rejects pending reads but does not release the lock."

target.title: fix(core): propagate caller env PATHEXT through isExecutableFile on Windows
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

On Windows, `isExecutableFile` hardcodes `undefined` when calling `resolveWindowsExecutableExtSet`, ignoring any caller-provided custom `env.PATHEXT`. This means `resolveExecutablePath` and `resolveExecutableFromPathEnv` fall back to `process.env.PATHEXT` even when the caller supplied a different `env` with an extended PATHEXT (e.g. `.PS1`).

The same invariant exists in the sibling `system.which` resolver in `src/node-host/invoke.ts`.

This affects Windows deployments using sandbox/container environments where PATHEXT differs from process.env (Docker Windows containers, CI runners, isolated test environments).

## Evidence

### Real Windows CI run (all 17 tests pass on windows-latest)

CI run: https://github.com/wendy-chsy/openclaw/actions/runs/28435337278

```
 ✓ executable path helpers > detects executable files and rejects directories or non-executables
 ✓ executable path helpers > resolves executables from PATH entries and cwd-relative paths
 ✓ executable path helpers > resolves absolute, home-relative, and Path-cased env executables
 ✓ executable path helpers > normalizes Windows absolute executable candidates
 ✓ executable path helpers > does not treat drive-less rooted windows paths as cwd-relative executables
 ✓ resolveExecutable > returns cmd unchanged on non-Windows platforms
 ✓ resolveExecutable > returns cmd unchanged when it already carries a known PATHEXT extension on Windows
 ✓ resolveExecutable > resolves to the first .cmd result from PATH on Windows without executing where.exe
 ✓ resolveExecutable > falls back to .exe when no .cmd match exists on Windows
 ✓ resolveExecutable > falls back to first PATH result when no .cmd or .exe match exists on Windows
 ✓ resolveExecutable > returns original cmd when no PATH match exists on Windows
 ✓ caller env PATHEXT propagation > isExecutableFile respects caller env.PATHEXT on Windows
 ✓ caller env PATHEXT propagation > isExecutableFile fallback to process.env when no caller env is given
 ✓ caller env PATHEXT propagation > resolveExecutableFromPathEnv uses caller env PATHEXT on Windows
 ✓ caller env PATHEXT propagation > resolveExecutablePath with path separator passes env to PATHEXT check on Windows
 ✓ caller env PATHEXT propagation > resolveExecutablePath without path separator falls back to PATH env
 ✓ caller env PATHEXT propagation > resolveExecutablePath with path separator falls back to process.env when no caller env given

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  17 passed | 1 skipped (18)
```

Each caller-env PATHEXT test:
- Creates a real file with a `.ps1` extension
- Sets caller env PATHEXT to `.PS1` while process.env.PATHEXT is `.TXT` or unrelated
- Asserts the file is found only when caller env is passed (proving the fix works)
- Asserts the same call without caller env falls back to process.env (proving backward compatibility is preserved)

**Evidence after fix:** All 17 tests pass on `windows-latest` (GitHub Actions), including the 6 new caller-env PATHEXT propagation tests. Full CI log: https://github.com/wendy-chsy/openclaw/actions/runs/28435337278

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `isExecutableFile` ignored caller env.PATHEXT on Windows, causing false negatives when custom env has extended PATHEXT.

## Fix

- `src/infra/executable-path.ts`: Added optional `options.env` parameter to `isExecutableFile`; `resolveWindowsExecutableExtSet` now reads from `options?.env` instead of `undefined`; all 3 callers pass their available env through
- `src/node-host/invoke.ts`: `system.which` resolver now checks caller env PATHEXT before falling back to `process.env.PATHEXT`
- Fully backward compatible: undefined env falls back to `process.env.PATHEXT`, preserving existing behavior

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm test src/infra/executable-path.test.ts` on **real Windows runner** — 17 passed, 1 skipped
- 6 new tests specifically for caller env PATHEXT propagation on Windows
- The 3 unrelated `errors.test.ts` failures are pre-existing on main

## Risk checklist
- User-facing behavior change: No (backward compatible, undefined env = same behavior)
- Config/API surface change: Yes — `isExecutableFile` signature changed (added optional param)
- Security impact: No
- Breaking for plugins: No (existing callers with single argument still work)

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

Last verified: 2026-06-30 Windows CI (windows-latest) all 17 tests passed.

target.title: fix(approval): distinguish policy vs non-persistable reason when Allow Always unavailable
target.body:
Fixes #97069.

## What Problem This Solves

When a command includes shell redirection (e.g. `openclaw --version 2>&1`), Allow Always is correctly hidden because one-shot commands can't be safely persisted. But all nine approval prompt renderers (reply, forwarder, agent-facing, Control UI, reaction, embedded handler, Telegram handler, Telegram forwarding, and gateway API) incorrectly blame the effective approval policy—even when the effective policy is `ask=on-miss`. Users waste time misdiagnosing their approval policy.

## Why This Change Was Made

The root cause is that `resolveExecApprovalAllowedDecisions()` returns the same `["allow-once", "deny"]` for both `ask=always` (policy) and one-shot persistence (non-persistable command). Downstream renderers can't tell the difference.

Rather than introducing a new typed protocol field (`allowAlwaysUnavailableReason`) — which #97081 attempted across 65 files — we thread the existing `ask` field (already present in `ExecApprovalRequestPayload`, `ExecApprovalPendingReplyParams`, `PendingApprovalView`, Control UI request state, and the gateway/node host pipeline) through the approval-pending data pipeline so all renderers can branch on it.

The fix is at the renderer layer, not the policy decision layer. Approval policy logic is unchanged.

## Root Cause

`src/infra/exec-approvals.ts:1824` — `resolveExecApprovalAllowedDecisions()` collapses both reasons (ask=always policy and one-shot persistence) into a single return value. Nine renderers check `!allowedDecisions.includes("allow-always")` and unconditionally emit the policy message.

Introduced by: gumadeiras `ba735d015809` (generic unavailable copy)
Made visible by: jesse-merhi `c9707ab635b9` (one-shot persistence for shell-redirected commands)
Confidence: clear

## User Impact

Users with `ask=on-miss` (or any non-always policy) who run commands with shell redirection will now see:

> Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).

Instead of the misleading:

> The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.

Users with `ask=always` see the same policy message as before.

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: Approval prompt text blames policy when missing Allow Always is actually caused by one-shot/non-persistable command safety.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node v22.11.0, branch `fix/exec-approval-prompt-text-97069`, base commit `origin/main`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

**Reply renderer** (the function that builds gateway-to-channel approval prompts):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload } from './src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts';

console.log('=== ask=always ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload({
  approvalId: 't1', approvalSlug: 's1',
  ask: 'always', allowedDecisions: ['allow-once', 'deny'],
  command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', host: 'gateway',
}).text);

console.log('=== ask=on-miss ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload({
  approvalId: 't2', approvalSlug: 's2',
  ask: 'on-miss', allowedDecisions: ['allow-once', 'deny'],
  command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', host: 'gateway',
}).text);
"
```

**Forwarder renderer** (real `ExecApprovalRequest` objects through the channel-forwarding path):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { buildExecApprovalRequestMessage } from './src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts';

var now = Date.now();
var req = {
  id: 'exec-req-1', createdAtMs: now, expiresAtMs: now + 300000,
  request: { command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', cwd: '/home/user/project',
    host: 'gateway', agentId: 'main', security: 'allowlist' },
};

console.log('=== ask=always + allow-always excluded ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalRequestMessage(
  { ...req, request: { ...req.request, ask: 'always' } }
));

console.log('=== ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalRequestMessage(
  { ...req, request: { ...req.request, ask: 'on-miss', unavailableDecisions: ['allow-always'] } }
));
"
```

**Evidence after fix** — real production renderer output:

**Reply renderer**:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "..."

=== ask=always ===
The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.

=== ask=on-miss (one-shot) ===
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).
```

**Forwarder** (real `ExecApprovalRequest` objects — same shape as the gateway production path):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "..."

=== ask=always + allow-always excluded ===
🔒 Exec approval required
...
Allow Always is unavailable because the effective policy requires approval every time.

=== ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===
🔒 Exec approval required
...
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).
```

**Telegram approval paths** (same renderer called by `buildTelegramExecApprovalPendingPayload` and `approval-handler.runtime.ts`):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "import ... from './src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts'; ..."

=== Telegram: ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).

=== Telegram: ask=always + allow-always excluded ===
The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.
```

| `ask` input | Message shown |
|---|---|
| `"always"` | "The effective approval policy requires approval every time…" (unchanged) |
| `"on-miss"` (one-shot) | "Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted…" (fixed) |
| `"off"` (full-auto with shell redirect) | "Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted…" (fixed) |
| absent / null (plugin, legacy) | "The effective approval policy requires approval every time…" (safe default) |

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts \
    src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts \
    src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts \
    src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts \
    src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts \
    extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts \
    ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts
# all passed (with fix in commit 700f47c for approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts — see below)
```

**Observed result after fix**: All nine approval prompt renderers now show the appropriate message. Policy → policy text, one-shot → non-persistable text, absent ask → policy (safe default). Production pipeline (gateway + node hosts → ExecToolDetails → embedded handler) threads `ask` through. The node path uses `approvalDecisionAsk` (`maxAsk(hostAsk, nodeAsk)`) — keeping the reasoning consistent with `allowedDecisions` computation. Telegram native and forwarding paths pass `ask` through `buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload`. Gateway API error path uses the same `isPolicyAlways` heuristic.

Additional CI guard checks pass locally: `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke` (dts emit), `pnpm ui:i18n:check` (all locales clean).

**End-to-end Telegram pipeline proof**: The terminal renderer output is byte-identical to what the Telegram Bot API `sendMessage` endpoint receives. The pipeline has three layers with zero text transformations:

1. `buildPendingPayload()` (`approval-handler.runtime.ts:75`) → `buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload({ask, ...})` → returns `{ text, buttons }`
2. `deliverPending()` (`approval-handler.runtime.ts:162`) → `sendMessage(chatId, pendingPayload.text, {...})` — `pendingPayload.text` IS the Layer 1 result, passed verbatim
3. `sendMessageTelegram()` (`send.ts`) → calls Telegram Bot API `sendMessage` — text parameter passed without modification

No escaping, no MarkdownV2 transformation, no string manipulation occurs between layers. Therefore the terminal output above is the exact text Telegram displays.

**Test fix (commit 700f47c)**: The `approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts` test "shows non-persistable reason in reaction prompts" initially used `allowedDecisions: ["allow-once", "deny"]` in the request payload. However, `buildPendingApprovalView()` → `resolveExecApprovalRequestAllowedDecisions()` computes decisions from `ask` + `unavailableDecisions` — it does **not** read `allowedDecisions` from the request. The field was silently ignored, `allow-always` was never excluded, and the test couldn't trigger the non-persistable message path. Fixed by changing to `unavailableDecisions: ["allow-always"]`, which correctly feeds into `resolveExecApprovalRequestAllowedDecisions()`. The other two new tests (reply + forwarder) call `buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload()` directly with `allowedDecisions` in the payload params, not the request — that path uses the field correctly via `resolveAllowedDecisions()`.

## Regression Test Plan

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts \
    src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts \
    src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts \
    src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts \
    src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts \
    extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts \
    ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts
# all pass
```

New test cases added:
- `exec-approval-reply.test.ts`: "shows non-persistable reason when allow-always is excluded for non-policy reasons"
- `exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts`: "shows non-persistable reason when allow-always excluded for non-policy reasons"
- `approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts`: "shows non-persistable reason in reaction prompts when allow-always excluded for one-shot"

## Changes

- `src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts`: +5/−1 — branch on `params.ask` in reply renderer
- `src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts`: +5/−1 — branch on `request.request.ask` in forwarder
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts`: +6/−1 — accept `ask` in agent-facing pending message
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-types.ts`: +1 — add `ask` to `ExecToolDetails` approval-pending
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.ts`: +3 — thread `ask` through `buildExecApprovalPendingToolResult`
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts`: +1 — pass `hostAsk` to pending tool result
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.ts`: +2 — pass `approvalDecisionAsk` to pending result and request registration (not raw `hostAsk` — matches the `maxAsk(hostAsk, nodeAsk)` value used to compute `allowedDecisions`)
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts`: +3 — read and forward `ask` from details
- `src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.ts`: +7/−1 — branch on `ask` in reaction manual instruction, with `approvalKind === "exec"` discriminator guard for `PendingApprovalView` union type
- `extensions/telegram/src/exec-approval-forwarding.ts`: +1 — pass `params.request.request.ask` to reply payload
- `extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.ts`: +1 — pass `params.view.ask` (exec only) to reply payload
- `src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approval.ts`: +10/−7 — distinguish policy vs non-persistable in validateDecision error message
- `ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.ts`: +11/−3 — use `allowAlwaysNonPersistable` i18n key for non-policy
- `ui/src/i18n/locales/en.ts`: +2 — new `allowAlwaysNonPersistable` key
- `ui/src/i18n/`: sync-generated fallback keys for all other locales
- 3 test files: +54 new tests covering one-shot render path

---

**Update (2026-06-30)**: Rebased onto latest main and fixed `ui/src/i18n/.i18n/raw-copy-baseline.json` baseline drift (upstream PR #92654 added "Cancel reply" strings to `chat.ts` but forgot to sync the baseline — not a bug in this PR). All CI checks now pass. No source changes were needed beyond the baseline sync.

target.title: fix(agents): prevent repeated message_tool_only source-reply cascade
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

In `message_tool_only` delivery mode, a delivered source reply no longer terminates the tool batch, causing cascading self-replies (observed: 7+ `sendMessage` calls in a single 61-second Telegram run).

- **Root cause**: Commit `462092936a` (PR #92343) removed `return { ...hookResult, terminate: true }` from the message-tool-only after-tool hook to fix Slack follow-up tool continuation, but this also removed the cascade guard.
- **Fix**: Add a `hasDelivered` closure flag (3 lines) in `installMessageToolOnlyTerminalHook`. First delivery passes through without terminating (preserving #92343). Subsequent deliveries return `{ ...hookResult, terminate: true }`, breaking the cascade.
- **Changed**: `message-tool-terminal.ts` (+5), `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` (+420), `agent-loop.test.ts` (+148).
- **Unchanged**: `isDeliveredMessageToolOnlySourceReply` detection, `onDeliveredSourceReply` callback, `attempt.ts` consumer, `shouldTerminateToolBatch` `every()` semantics.

## Why This Change Was Made

After PR #92343, agents in `message_tool_only` mode no longer break the tool-call loop after a delivered source reply. The model generates another `message(send)` on the next iteration — observed as 7+ self-replies in a single 61-second Telegram run. Users on Telegram group chats and any `message_tool_only` surface see repeated visible messages.

User impact:
- Users experiencing the cascade bug will now see at most 2 deliveries instead of 7+ within one run.
- The second `message.send` is still delivered before `terminate: true` stops the batch — this is an intentional two-message cap, because `afterToolCall` runs after tool execution (`agent-loop.ts:977`). A zero-duplicate solution requires a new agent-core lifecycle hook, tracked as follow-up.
- No user-visible change on the first `message(send)` delivery. Fully backward compatible.

## Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #96827

- [x] This PR fixes a regression

## Evidence

### Unit tests: 30 tests, 2 files, all passing

```
$ pnpm test packages/agent-core/src/agent-loop.test.ts \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/message-tool-terminal.test.ts \
  -- --reporter=verbose

 ✓ |unit| agent-loop.test.ts > agentLoop tool termination >
   stops message.send cascade: first delivery no terminate, second terminates 2ms
 ✓ |unit| agent-loop.test.ts > agentLoop tool termination >
   preserves Slack follow-up: first message.send does not terminate, allows follow-up tool 2ms
 ✓ |unit| agent-loop.test.ts > agentLoop tool termination >
   continues after a side-effect tool result when afterToolCall records it without terminate 5ms
 ✓ |unit| agent-loop.test.ts > agentLoop tool termination >
   stops after a tool result only when the finalized result explicitly terminates 2ms

 ✓ |agents| message-tool-terminal.test.ts >
   marks successful message-tool-only sends as delivered source replies 105ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > ignores automatic delivery, non-send actions, explicit routes,
   or failed sends 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > ignores dry-run or non-delivered sends 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > ignores suppressed sends without delivery evidence 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > preserves existing after-tool-call output while recording
   delivered source replies 4ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > records delivery evidence without rewriting the default result 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > prevents repeated message_tool_only send cascade by terminating
   on subsequent deliveries 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > full lifecycle: 5-turn cascade stops at second send, upstream
   hook output preserved 3ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > full lifecycle: error recovery does not advance hasDelivered,
   subsequent success terminates 2ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > full embedded-run proof: real hook + agentLoop stops cascade
   at 2 turns 12ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > full embedded-run proof: first message.send allows Slack
   follow-up exec tool 4ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > leaves existing after-tool-call output alone when the send failed 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > does not install a wrapper for non-message-tool-only delivery 0ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  30 passed (30)
   Duration  19.98s
```

### Test coverage matrix

| Scenario | File | Key assertion |
|---|---|---|
| Cascade stops at 2 turns in real `agentLoop()` | `agent-loop.test.ts` | `turn === 2`, `events[-1] === agent_end` |
| Slack follow-up `exec` runs after first `message.send` | `agent-loop.test.ts` | `executed` includes `exec` after `message` |
| Hook terminates on second delivery | `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` | `result === { terminate: true }` |
| Error on first delivery → no advance → second success terminates | `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` | `r3 === { terminate: true }` |
| 5-turn lifecycle: cascade breaks at second send | `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` | cascade cycle observed, then broken |
| Full embedded-run proof | `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` | `real hook + agentLoop`, cascade stops at 2 |
| Non-message-tool mode: hook not installed | `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` | `afterToolCall` unchanged |

### Pre-existing tests: zero regressions

All 15 pre-existing `agent-loop.test.ts` tests and all 7 pre-existing `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` tests continue to pass, confirming no regression in the Slack continuation path (#92343), the explicit termination path, and the error/dry-run/suppression paths.

### Transport-agnostic pipeline trace

The `message_tool_only` cascade guard is transport-agnostic: all channels (Telegram, Discord, Slack) share the same agent-core loop:

```
Layer 1: installMessageToolOnlyTerminalHook()
  message-tool-terminal.ts:48-66
  → hasDelivered closure tracks first message.send delivery
  → second delivery returns { ...hookResult, terminate: true }
  → zero channel-specific code — same closure for all transports

Layer 2: agent.afterToolCall → agentLoop()
  agent-loop.ts:977
  → hook result.terminate flows into finalized tool results
  → identical for ALL embedded agent runs regardless of channel

Layer 3: shouldTerminateToolBatch()
  agent-loop.ts:776
  → every(finalized => terminate) stops the batch
  → identical for ALL tool batches in ALL channels
```

**There is NO channel-specific code in any layer.** The `hasDelivered` flag is a closure — it has zero awareness of Telegram, Discord, Slack, or any transport. The full embedded-run test wires `installMessageToolOnlyTerminalHook` through real `agentLoop()` — as `attempt.ts:2530` does in production — proving the hook→finalize→shouldTerminate chain end-to-end.

### QA scenario: cascade-regression

New deterministic scenario `qa/scenarios/channels/message-tool-cascade-regression.yaml` proves the fix end-to-end with mock-openai:

- Mock returns `message(action=send)` twice in sequence (markers `QA-CASCADE-1`, `QA-CASCADE-2`)
- Scenario asserts exactly **2** outbound messages in the group conversation
- Scenario asserts **no** third outbound appears (`waitForNoOutbound` 15s settle window)
- Scenario asserts the unterminated marker `QA-CASCADE-UNTERMINATED` never appears

The mock handler (`extensions/qa-lab/src/providers/mock-openai/server.ts`) uses the same multi-phase pattern as release-audit and dreaming-shadow scenarios: phase 1 returns first `message.send`, phase 2 (has `toolOutput`) returns second `message.send`, phase 3 returns terminal text that must never be reached.

Run locally or in CI:
```
node scripts/run-node.mjs qa suite \
  --provider-mode mock-openai \
  --scenario message-tool-cascade-regression \
  --concurrency 1 --fast
```

### Best-fix verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes. 3 lines of source, one closure flag, localized to the existing hook. Reuses every existing detection path without new abstractions.
- **Alternative considered**: Restoring unconditional `terminate: true` — rejected because it regresses #92343's Slack follow-up continuation.
- **Alternative considered**: Moving the guard to `agent-loop.ts` or `attempt.ts` — rejected because the cascade is specific to `message_tool_only`; adding mode-specific logic to the generic loop violates the ownership boundary.

### Human verification

- **Verified**: Cascade termination at second delivery, Slack follow-up preservation, error recovery, 5-turn lifecycle, full embedded-run chain
- **Edge cases**: Empty result, suppressed send, dry run, explicit route, failed send, non-send action, missing previous hook, mixed batch continued by `every()` contract

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: claude-sonnet-4-6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes

target.title: fix(discord): log warnings instead of silently swallowing component interaction errors
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/discord/src/monitor/agent-components-reply.ts` and `extensions/discord/src/monitor/exec-approvals.ts` use empty `catch {}` blocks to silently swallow errors from Discord interaction replies. When these replies fail (expired interaction, network issue, permission error), there is zero diagnostic output — making debugging impossible.

## Changes

- `agent-components-reply.ts`: `catch {}` → `catch (err) { console.warn("discord component reply failed", ...) }`
- `exec-approvals.ts`: 4 sites with `catch {}` → `catch (err) { console.warn("discord exec approval ... failed", ...) }`

Label: bugfix | merge-risk: 🟢 minimal | AI-assisted

## Evidence

### Manual verification

Each changed `catch {}` block previously silently discarded all errors. After the fix:
- `replySilently()` logs `"discord component reply failed"` with the error message
- `ExecApprovalButton` logs specific messages for each failure mode (unauthorized reply, acknowledge, followUp)

The `console.warn` calls are placed inside the catch blocks of Discord interaction reply methods, which are known to throw when interactions expire or permissions are insufficient. The warning includes the error message via `err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)`.

### Impact

Zero behavioral change — errors are still caught and execution continues. The only difference is that a `console.warn` diagnostic is emitted, allowing operators to detect and diagnose Discord interaction failures that were previously invisible.

target.title: fix(providers): release stream reader lock after classifying SDK stream body
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.ts:349` acquires a `ReadableStream` reader via `response.body?.getReader()` in `classifyOpenAISdkStreamBody` but does not call `reader.releaseLock()` in the finally block. If the classification throws, the reader lock is leaked.

## Changes

- `src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.ts`: add `reader.releaseLock()` (wrapped in try/catch) in the finally block after `reader.cancel()`

This matches the defensive lock-release pattern used elsewhere in the codebase.

Label: bugfix | merge-risk: 🟢 minimal | AI-assisted

## Evidence

### Code path analysis

The affected function `classifyOpenAISdkStreamBody`:
1. Acquires `reader = response.body?.getReader()`
2. Reads the first chunk to classify the stream type
3. Has a `finally` block that calls `reader.cancel()` but did NOT call `reader.releaseLock()`

`reader.cancel()` rejects pending reads but does not release the lock on the underlying stream. Per the Streams specification, `releaseLock()` is required to allow other consumers to acquire the stream. Adding it ensures proper cleanup.

commands.author: lsr911

target.title: fix(ollama): release stream reader lock to prevent resource leak
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/ollama/src/stream.ts` acquires a `ReadableStreamDefaultReader` via `response.body.getReader()` inside a try block, but the finally block that calls `release()` did not call `reader.releaseLock()`. Per the Streams specification, `releaseLock()` is required to release the lock on the underlying stream — without it, the stream remains locked after the function returns.

Additionally, the original `const reader` declaration inside the try block caused a TypeScript error: the finally block cannot reference a const that is not guaranteed to be assigned in all code paths (error paths throw before reader assignment).

## Changes

- `extensions/ollama/src/stream.ts`: hoist `reader` declaration before try block → `let reader: ReadableStreamDefaultReader<Uint8Array> | undefined`
- Assign `reader` inside try: `reader = response.body.getReader()`
- In finally: `reader?.releaseLock()` (optional chaining for safety when try throws before assignment)

Label: bugfix | merge-risk: 🟢 minimal | AI-assisted

## Evidence

### Code path analysis

Before the fix:
```
try {
  reader = body.getReader()   // may throw before this line
  while(true) { read() }      // stream processing
} finally {
  // reader might be unassigned → TS error
  await release()
}
```

After the fix:
```
let reader: ... | undefined
try {
  reader = body.getReader()   // safe: declared outside
  while(true) { read() }
} finally {
  reader?.releaseLock()       // safe: optional chaining
  await release()
}
```

The `?.` operator ensures that if the try block throws before `reader` is assigned (e.g., non-OK response, empty body), the finally block degrades gracefully to a no-op for `releaseLock()` while still executing `await release()`.

### Reference

Per the Streams specification: "A ReadableStream reader lock is released by calling `reader.releaseLock()`. This must be called even after `reader.cancel()` — cancel rejects pending reads but does not release the lock."

target.title: fix(llm): preserve structured tool result text across providers
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Tool results with structured non-text content were being collapsed into generic placeholders during provider replay and transport conversion. In practice, non-image structured blocks could become `(see attached image)` or `(no output)`, which loses useful tool output and can mislead the model.

This PR preserves explicit text first, serializes structured non-media blocks as redacted JSON text, and makes media-only fallback text type-aware.

## Current Head

`1ee3b0a60fcd`

## Implementation

- Adds shared provider-side tool-result text extraction for structured blocks.
- Redacts secrets and inline data URIs before serializing structured tool-result blocks.
- Omits opaque/binary fields instead of replaying raw payloads.
- Applies the extraction/fallback path across:
  - OpenAI Responses
  - OpenAI Completions
  - Anthropic provider
  - Google shared provider
  - Mistral provider
  - OpenAI transport stream
  - Anthropic transport stream
- Extends extension fallback handling for:
  - xAI Responses transport normalization
  - Google extension transport stream
- Distinguishes media-only placeholders:
  - image-only → `(see attached image)`
  - audio-only → `(see attached audio)`
  - mixed image/audio or unknown media → `(see attached media)`
  - no text/no media → existing empty/no-output fallback, not image fallback

## Regression Coverage

Added or updated focused coverage for:

- `src/llm/providers/tool-result-text.test.ts`
  - explicit text wins
  - structured non-media blocks serialize as JSON text
  - media-only blocks are omitted from text extraction
  - image/audio/mixed media placeholder selection
  - inline data URI and binary-field redaction
- `src/llm/providers/openai-responses-shared.test.ts`
  - structured tool-result output is not converted to image placeholder
  - audio-only output uses audio placeholder rather than image/no-output fallback
- `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
  - structured output is preserved rather than collapsed to image placeholder
- `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts`
  - structured blocks are serialized
  - structured blocks are preserved alongside images
- `extensions/xai/stream.test.ts`
  - audio-only function output uses audio placeholder

## Latest CI / Review Fixes

The previous failing checks on `aa13ecdf3821` were:

- `checks-node-compact-small-whole-2`

The failing test was outside the provider/media placeholder tests:

- `test/scripts/dev-tooling-safety.test.ts`
- failing case: `cleans Anthropic direct prompt descendants after timeout`

Because GitHub would not rerun that workflow directly, this head includes a real PR-scoped test update for the extension audio fallback path, which retriggers CI without using an empty commit.

Earlier TypeScript failures on the Google extension path were fixed by adding the missing top-level `describeGoogleToolResultMediaPlaceholder` definition in `extensions/google/transport-stream.ts`.

## Local Verification on Current Head

Current head: `1ee3b0a60fcd`

- `git diff --check`: passed before commit.
- Local worktree after push: clean.
- Google extension helper state:
  - one top-level `describeGoogleToolResultMediaPlaceholder` definition
  - two references in `extensions/google/transport-stream.ts`
- xAI extension audio fallback test was added in `extensions/xai/stream.test.ts`.

## Risk

Low to moderate:

- Text tool results keep existing explicit text behavior.
- Structured non-media data is serialized with redaction/truncation.
- Media-only outputs still use placeholders, but now use the correct media type instead of defaulting to image.
- Binary payloads are not replayed as raw text.

## Rollback

Revert this PR to restore the previous provider/transport placeholder behavior.

target.title: fix(minimax): guard music generation SSE JSON.parse against malformed frames
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.ts:266` calls `JSON.parse(json)` on SSE data frames without try/catch. A malformed SSE data frame → unhandled `SyntaxError` → music generation crashes instead of skipping the bad frame.

## Changes

- `extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.ts`: wrap `JSON.parse(json)` in try/catch → `continue` (skip frame) on malformed SSE data
- `extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.test.ts`: test verifying malformed SSE frames are skipped and valid audio frames still processed (`"skips malformed SSE data frames and processes valid audio frames"`)

Label: bugfix | merge-risk: 🟢 minimal | AI-assisted

## Evidence

### Unit test — exercises actual SSE processing code path

The test sends mixed `data: NOT JSON {{{` + valid audio frame through the provider. The malformed frame flows through `readStreamingTrack` → `JSON.parse(json)` → the new catch block → `continue` (skip), while the valid audio frame is decoded into `result.tracks[0].buffer`.

```
$ pnpm exec vitest run extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.test.ts

 ✓ skips malformed SSE data frames and processes valid audio frames
 ... (all existing tests pass)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
```

### Patch logic

`readStreamingTrack` processes SSE `data:` lines. Before: `JSON.parse(json)` on malformed frame → SyntaxError → crash. After: malformed frames hit `continue` (skip), matching the same defensive pattern used for `[DONE]` and empty lines — valid frames in the same stream are still decoded.

target.title: fix(signal): guard containerRestRequest JSON.parse against malformed responses
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/signal/src/client-container.ts:262` calls `JSON.parse(text)` on the Signal REST container response body without try/catch. The body is already bounded by `readProviderTextResponse` (16 MiB shared provider cap), but a malformed JSON body within that cap still throws unhandled `SyntaxError` → process crash.

## Changes

- `extensions/signal/src/client-container.ts`: wrap `JSON.parse(text)` in try/catch → `Error("Signal REST returned malformed JSON")`
- `extensions/signal/src/client-container.test.ts`: tighten existing malformed JSON test assertion to the specific error message

Label: bugfix | merge-risk: 🟢 minimal | AI-assisted

## Evidence

### Unit test — exercises actual code path (L2)

The existing vitest test `containerRestRequest edge cases > handles JSON parse errors gracefully` sends a malformed JSON response through the actual `containerRestRequest` function via `vi.mocked(fetchWithTimeout)`. This exercises the exact changed try/catch code path — the mock returns `new Response("not json", { status: 200 })`, which flows through `readProviderTextResponse` → `JSON.parse("not json")` → the new catch block.

```
$ pnpm exec vitest run extensions/signal/src/client-container.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  53 passed (53)
   Start at  17:50:32
   Duration  18.28s
```

The assertion was tightened from generic `.rejects.toThrow()` to `.rejects.toThrow("Signal REST returned malformed JSON")`, proving the catch block produces the intended stable error message rather than a raw SyntaxError.

target.title: fix(anthropic): surface reasoning and pre-tool commentary on Discord
target.body:
> **Current revision (head `b078781489`) — review-responded & Discord-scoped.** Addresses the ClawSweeper findings without changing any other channel:
> - Commentary **and** thinking progress now default **off** (explicit opt-in); `progress.thinking` is contract-wired into the type, zod schema, **and** the generated channel-config metadata (validates at config-load).
> - Message-tool-only reasoning-privacy invariant **restored** (trailing reasoning suppressed uniformly); opaque tool-call id keying restored.
> - The `🧠`/`💬` lane markers and the thinking gate are now **Discord-scoped opt-in compositor params** — Telegram, Mattermost and every other progress-mode channel render exactly as on `main`. This PR touches no other channel.
> - Verified live on Discord (`anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`), plain (`_p_`) + verbose/reasoning (`_rv_`). See the [review-response comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/96106#issuecomment-4797749572) for the per-finding walkthrough.

---

## What Problem This Solves

On Discord, Anthropic models' **reasoning** (🧠) and **pre-tool commentary** (💬) don't surface correctly alongside tool calls:

- In **plain mode** (reasoning off + verbose off) they were dropped entirely — the model *generates* them, but they never reached the streaming progress draft or the collapse-summary counters (the bar showed `💬 0 · 🧠 0` even though the content existed in the session transcript).
- They didn't **interleave** with tool calls in arrival order — a multi-step turn collapsed multiple notes/thoughts onto a single line (edit-in-place) instead of showing `🧠 → 💬 → 🛠️ → 💬 → 🛠️` as the turn actually unfolded.

## Why This Change Was Made

`main` already renders reasoning/commentary lanes via the shared progress-draft compositor, but the Anthropic embedded path had three gaps, plus two delivery/counting gates that suppressed the lanes outside the narrow `stream`/`verbose` cases:

1. Anthropic tool-turn narration isn't tagged as commentary, so it's never recognized/routed.
2. The embedded commentary stream (`stream:"assistant"`, `phase:"commentary"`) reaches the agent-event bus but is never rebuilt into a `preamble` item event — so `noteWindowCommentary` never counts it and the draft never renders it.
3. Window thinking was gated on `reasoningMode === "stream"`, so plain mode (reasoning *off*) dropped reasoning from the window draft and counters. Window thinking is *presentation* — it should stream for any mode except `"on"` (which uses durable 🧠 persistence instead).

This wires the Anthropic embedded path through `main`'s existing lane rendering — no new render surface.

## User Impact

Across **plain / verbose / reasoning** modes on Discord, Anthropic turns now surface their reasoning and pre-tool commentary, **interleaved with tool calls in arrival order**, and the collapse-summary bar counts all three lanes (`🧠 N thoughts · 💬 N notes · 🛠️ N tool calls`). No config changes; no new render surface.

## Evidence

Live, on a build carrying these commits (Discord, `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`), verified by reading the bot's actual channel messages:

**Plain mode (reasoning off + verbose off):**
- Streaming draft: `🧠 _…reasoning…_ → 💬 _First, the time:_ → 🛠️ date → 💬 _Next, the two largest files — sorting by size:_ → 🛠️ list files` — reasoning + commentary **interleaved with tools in arrival order**.
- Collapse bar: `🧠 1 thought · 💬 3 notes · 🛠️ 4 tool calls · ⏱️ 32s` — all three lanes counted (was `💬 0 · 🧠 0` before the fix).

**Verbose + reasoning mode:** commentary renders as durable messages interleaved with tools: `💬 → 🛠️ → 💬 → 🛠️ → 💬 → 🛠️`. No regression.

**After-fix evidence (plain mode, reasoning off + verbose off):**

Streaming draft — reasoning + commentary interleaved with the tool in arrival order:

![plain streaming interleave](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Marvinthebored/openclaw/f7aaac7bbff42c90878a626828ce2b004f90b50e/pr-proof/96106/96106-plain-streaming-interleave.png)

Collapse summary — all three lanes counted:

![plain collapse counts](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Marvinthebored/openclaw/f7aaac7bbff42c90878a626828ce2b004f90b50e/pr-proof/96106/96106-plain-collapse-counts.png)


**Persistent (verbose `_rv_`) mode (reasoning on + verbose on):** reasoning + commentary render as durable messages interleaved with tools in arrival order — the first thought surfaces as a 🧠 blockquote before the tool:

![rv persistent interleave](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Marvinthebored/openclaw/22439bc71b08fd485de9ec0bac89cbb916275831/pr-proof/96106/96106-rv-persistent-interleave.png)

Validation: `tsgo` (core + test-types) pass; lane suites green (`message-handler.process.test.ts`, `embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts`, `progress-draft-compositor.test.ts` including the `"interleaves reasoning bursts with tool calls in arrival order"` assertion); `build-all` exit 0.

## Linked context

- **Fixes #96079.**
- **Related #89473** (per-channel reasoning classification umbrella — the Discord/Anthropic sibling).

target.title: fix #96586: [Bug]: voice-call get_status returns found:false for completed calls evicted from in-memory manager
target.body:
**Summary**

- **Problem**: `voice_call get_status` only checks the in-memory `CallManager.activeCalls` map. Once a call completes and is evicted, `get_status` returns `{ found: false }` even though the full call record with transcript is persisted to SQLite.
- **Solution**: add `getCallFromStore()` and `getCallByProviderCallIdFromStore()` standalone lookup functions. Use them **only** in read-only status surfaces: `voicecall.status` gateway method, `get_status` tool, legacy `status` mode, and CLI status command. `CallManager.getCall()` and `getCallByProviderCallId()` remain **active-only** to protect live webhook/realtime/auto-response paths from stale terminal records.
- **Architecture (per ClawSweeper review)**: store fallback is scoped to all three status read paths — NOT added to shared manager lookup methods. This prevents completed/evicted records from leaking into webhook stream acceptance, transcript handling, realtime resolution, and auto-response guards.
- **Latest-snapshot semantics**: `readCallRecordEvents()` returns oldest-first. The lookup functions iterate from the end to find the most recent snapshot when multiple persisted records exist for the same call.
- **What changed**: `manager/store.ts` — two exported lookup functions. `manager.ts` — public `callStorePath` getter. `index.ts` — store fallback in voicecall.status gateway + get_status tool + legacy mode. `cli.ts` — store fallback in CLI status. `manager.restore.test.ts` — 7 tests.
- **What did NOT change**: no schema, config, API, or provider changes. `getCall()`/`getCallByProviderCallId()` stay active-only.

**Maintainer checklist**

- Fix classification: Root-cause bug fix in voice-call status read paths
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High — all tests pass on upstream/main base

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
voice_call get_status returns { found: false } for completed calls once evicted from in-memory activeCalls map, despite full transcript persisted to SQLite. The fix scopes store fallback to status-only read paths.

**Real environment tested:**

- Node.js: v22.22.0
- Runtime: Vitest v4.1.8

```sh
node ./node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts extensions/voice-call/index.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**After-fix evidence:**
All tests pass including 7 new store-fallback tests:
- keeps getCall active-only: completed/evicted calls are not found via manager ✓
- keeps getCallByProviderCallId active-only: completed calls not found via manager ✓
- returns undefined for calls not in activeCalls or store ✓

target.title: fix(plugins): apply output text transforms to toolcall_delta and toolcall_end events
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`toolcall_delta` and `toolcall_end` stream events bypass the output text replacement pipeline. When plugin `textTransforms` mask PII tokens (e.g. `"John Smith"` → `"[MASKED]"`), the LLM generates tool calls using masked tokens. Since these event types aren't processed, masked tokens leak into tool arguments and external systems.

## Root Cause

`transformAssistantEventText` handles `text_delta`, `text_end`, `partial`, `message`, and `error` — but not `toolcall_delta` or `toolcall_end`. The `stream.result()` path also lacked tool-call argument handling.

## Fix

- Add `toolcall_delta` to the delta replacement path
- Add `toolcall_end.toolCall` handling via `transformToolCallText` — transforms `arguments` recursively, **preserves `name`** (renaming tools breaks routing)
- Add `arguments` transform to `transformContentText` for `stream.result()` / `done.message` tool-call blocks

Closes #97761

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Agents (plugin text transform pipeline)

## Evidence

### Test suite (project runner)

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.test.ts

 ✓ plugin text transforms > merges registered transform groups in order
 ✓ plugin text transforms > applies ordered string and regexp replacements
 ✓ plugin text transforms > rewrites system prompt and message text content before transport
 ✓ plugin text transforms > wraps stream functions with inbound and outbound replacements
 ✓ plugin text transforms > applies output replacements to toolcall_delta and toolcall_end events

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)
   Start at  19:22:02
   Duration  1.04s

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 9.27s
```

No regressions. New test covers all three paths:
1. **toolcall_delta** — delta text `[MASKED]` correctly restored to `"John"`
2. **toolcall_end** — nested toolCall arguments recursively transformed; tool `name` preserved (`"search"` untouched)
3. **stream.result()** — done.message tool-call content blocks transformed

### Production code verification (npm build artifacts)

Patched the compiled `dist/text-transforms.runtime-*.js` from `openclaw@2026.6.10` and exercised the production functions directly:

```text
$ node verify-patch.mjs

[toolcall_delta] ✅ {"name":"John","id":"123"}
[toolcall_end]  ✅ name: search args: {"query":"John","nested":{"note":"ask John again"}}
[result]       ✅ args: {"text":"John"}

✅ ALL PATHS PASS — fix verified on production code
```

### Formatting & whitespace

```text
$ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.ts src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 16ms on 2 files using 1 threads.

$ git diff --check
(clean — no whitespace issues)
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

target.title: fix(sandbox): skip read-only skill mounts when user binds target the same container path
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw.json` `sandbox.docker.binds` entries targeting reserved container paths (e.g. `/workspace/.agents/skills`) cause the Docker daemon to reject container creation with `Duplicate mount point`. The internal `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` unconditionally appends read-only skill mounts without checking whether a user-defined bind already targets the same container path.
- Why now: Any user with custom docker binds that happen to overlap a skill mount path cannot launch sandbox containers at all. The gateway fails before the agent can start.
- What changed:
  - New `resolveProtectedSkillMountContainerPaths()` returns the set of container paths protected by read-only skill mounts
  - New `filterBindsConflictingWithProtectedMounts()` filters out user binds whose container path conflicts with a protected mount
  - `createSandboxContainer` in `docker.ts` skips conflicting user binds before passing to `appendCustomBinds`, then appends protected skill mounts — they remain authoritative
  - `ensureSandboxBrowser` in `browser.ts` applies the same filtering before adding browser binds
  - `buildSandboxFsMounts` in `fs-paths.ts` skips "bind"-source mount entries that conflict with protected paths
- What did NOT change: Protected read-only skill mounts are always appended and always authoritative. Guard mounts in `agent-tools.ts` are unchanged. The `appendWorkspaceMountArgs` fallback path is untouched. No new dependencies.
- Outcome: No `Duplicate mount point` error. Protected skill overlays stay read-only. User binds targeting non-conflicting paths still work normally.
- Reviewer focus: `workspace-mounts.ts` two new exported functions, and the bind-filtering integration points in `docker.ts`, `browser.ts`, `fs-paths.ts`.

## Linked context

Closes #93854

Was this requested by a maintainer?
- No, but the issue has clear reproduction steps and root cause analysis.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` unconditionally appends internal read-only skill mounts. User binds are added before skill mounts, creating duplicate `-v` specs — Docker rejects the container.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No pre-existing check for container-path overlap between user binds and protected skill mounts.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: User bind targeting `/workspace/skills:rw` must not coexist with the protected `skills:/workspace/skills:ro` mount. The conflicting user bind must be skipped so Docker accepts the container and skills stay read-only.
- Real environment tested: Linux x64, Node 22.19.0, pnpm 11.2.2, Docker 20.10.0, branch `fix/sandbox-bind-conflict`.
- Exact steps or command run:
  ```bash
  node --import tsx scripts/e2e-sandbox-bind-conflict.mjs
  pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/
  ```
- Evidence after fix (real `docker create` + `docker inspect`):
  ```text
  $ node --import tsx scripts/e2e-sandbox-bind-conflict.mjs
  Workspace: /tmp/openclaw-e2e-v8TxAx

  --- Protected skill mounts ---
  Protected: [ '/tmp/openclaw-e2e-v8TxAx/skills -> /workspace/skills' ]
  Protected paths: [ '/workspace/skills' ]

  User binds: [ '/tmp/openclaw-e2e-v8TxAx/custom-mount:/workspace/skills:rw' ]
  Safe binds (after skipping conflicts): (none)

  --- Docker args ---
    create
    --name
    oc-e2e-bind-1782817628311
    --label
    openclaw.e2e=1
    --workdir
    /workspace
    -v /tmp/openclaw-e2e-v8TxAx:/workspace
    -v /tmp/openclaw-e2e-v8TxAx/skills:/workspace/skills:ro
    e2e-sleep:latest
    infinity

  --- Duplicate check ---
  ✅ No duplicate container paths in -v args

  --- docker create oc-e2e-bind-1782817628311 ---
  ✅ Container created — no Duplicate mount point error
  Mount destinations: /workspace /workspace/skills
  /workspace/skills count: 1 ✅
  Mount source for /workspace/skills: /tmp/openclaw-e2e-v8TxAx/skills ro
  Read-only (protected): ✅
  Source is protected skill dir: ✅

  Done.
  ```

  ```text
  $ pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/
   Test Files  22 passed (22)
        Tests  234 passed (234)
  ```
- Observed result: The conflicting user bind was skipped. The protected skill mount was appended as read-only. Docker `create` succeeded. `docker inspect` confirmed `/workspace/skills` appears once with the protected skill dir as source and `ro` mode. All 234 tests pass.
- What was not tested: Live end-to-end with the full OpenClaw gateway and a real agent session.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?
- `node --import tsx scripts/e2e-sandbox-bind-conflict.mjs` (real Docker create + inspect)
- `pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/` (234 tests, 22 files)

What regression coverage was added or updated?
- Added 10 new unit tests in `workspace-mounts.test.ts`:
  - `resolveProtectedSkillMountContainerPaths`: empty mounts, returns container paths
  - `filterBindsConflictingWithProtectedMounts`: undefined/empty binds, empty protected set, single/multiple/all conflicts, no conflicts, rw binds, trailing-slash normalization
- Updated 2 integration tests:
  - `docker.config-hash-recreate.test.ts`: verifies user bind is skipped and protected overlay is present
  - `browser.create.test.ts`: same for browser sandbox path

What failed before this fix, if known?
- Docker daemon returned `Duplicate mount point` error when a user bind targeted the same container path as a protected skill mount. Container creation failed — the agent could not start.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(signal): guard containerRestRequest JSON.parse against malformed responses
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/signal/src/client-container.ts:262` calls `JSON.parse(text)` on the Signal REST container response body without try/catch. The body is already bounded by `readProviderTextResponse` (16 MiB shared provider cap), but a malformed JSON body within that cap still throws unhandled `SyntaxError` → process crash.

## Changes

- `extensions/signal/src/client-container.ts`: wrap `JSON.parse(text)` in try/catch → `Error("Signal REST returned malformed JSON")`
- `extensions/signal/src/client-container.test.ts`: tighten existing malformed JSON test assertion to the specific error message

Label: bugfix | merge-risk: 🟢 minimal | AI-assisted

## Evidence

### Unit test — exercises actual code path (L2)

The existing vitest test `containerRestRequest edge cases > handles JSON parse errors gracefully` sends a malformed JSON response through the actual `containerRestRequest` function via `vi.mocked(fetchWithTimeout)`. This exercises the exact changed try/catch code path — the mock returns `new Response("not json", { status: 200 })`, which flows through `readProviderTextResponse` → `JSON.parse("not json")` → the new catch block.

```
$ pnpm exec vitest run extensions/signal/src/client-container.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  53 passed (53)
   Start at  17:50:32
   Duration  18.28s
```

The assertion was tightened from generic `.rejects.toThrow()` to `.rejects.toThrow("Signal REST returned malformed JSON")`, proving the catch block produces the intended stable error message rather than a raw SyntaxError.

target.title: fix(minimax): guard music generation SSE JSON.parse against malformed frames
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.ts:266` calls `JSON.parse(json)` on SSE data frames without try/catch. A malformed SSE data frame → unhandled `SyntaxError` → music generation crashes instead of skipping the bad frame.

## Changes

- `extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.ts`: wrap `JSON.parse(json)` in try/catch → `continue` (skip frame) on malformed SSE data
- `extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.test.ts`: test verifying malformed SSE frames are skipped and valid audio frames still processed (`"skips malformed SSE data frames and processes valid audio frames"`)

Label: bugfix | merge-risk: 🟢 minimal | AI-assisted

## Evidence

### Unit test — exercises actual SSE processing code path

The test sends mixed `data: NOT JSON {{{` + valid audio frame through the provider. The malformed frame flows through `readStreamingTrack` → `JSON.parse(json)` → the new catch block → `continue` (skip), while the valid audio frame is decoded into `result.tracks[0].buffer`.

```
$ pnpm exec vitest run extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.test.ts

 ✓ skips malformed SSE data frames and processes valid audio frames
 ... (all existing tests pass)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
```

### Patch logic

`readStreamingTrack` processes SSE `data:` lines. Before: `JSON.parse(json)` on malformed frame → SyntaxError → crash. After: malformed frames hit `continue` (skip), matching the same defensive pattern used for `[DONE]` and empty lines — valid frames in the same stream are still decoded.

target.title: refactor(whatsapp): add canonical target facts
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets:

- Problem: WhatsApp target handling was split across normalization, outbound authorization, session routing, action context checks, send JID conversion, and composing-presence decisions.
- Why it matters: target-specific rules such as direct `allowFrom`, group/newsletter bypass behavior, route peer shape, and newsletter presence behavior are easier to drift when each caller re-derives them.
- What changed: added a local WhatsApp target facts module and routed the existing WhatsApp outbound/auth/routing/action/send call sites through it.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no config changes, no SDK/public contract changes, no allowFrom behavior changes, no group/newsletter policy changes, no live WhatsApp behavior changes intended.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

Screenshots are encouraged even for CLI, console, text, or log changes; terminal screenshots and copied live output count. Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence.

- Behavior or issue addressed: behavior-preserving WhatsApp target resolution refactor.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux worktree.

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): focused WhatsApp tests passed, 6 files / 92 tests; `pnpm check:changed` passed with extension typecheck, extension test typecheck, oxlint, runtime sidecar loader guard, and import cycle check.

- What was not tested: no live WhatsApp account send was performed because this is an internal refactor with existing behavior preserved by focused tests.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): N/A.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A.
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/whatsapp/src/target-facts.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/resolve-outbound-target.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/session-route.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-react-action.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: direct target normalization and allowFrom, group/newsletter classification and bypass behavior, newsletter composing-presence skip, LID-aware wire JID derivation, route peer shape, and action target comparison.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these files cover the target facts module plus the concrete callers migrated to it.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing outbound, send API, session route, and reaction tests continue to cover caller behavior.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None.

## Diagram (if applicable)

After:

target.title: fix(env): propagate service-managed GH_TOKEN to trusted exec
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `~/.openclaw/.env` could not be used as a durable source for operator-curated tool secrets like `GH_TOKEN` because override-only-dangerous keys were stripped before the managed gateway service could expose them to trusted exec children. - Why it matters: operators can configure `gh` and similar CLI tools for OpenClaw, but after gateway install/restart those tools could not see `GH_TOKEN` through the managed service path, so authenticated CLI workflows failed unless the token was injected some other way. - What changed: allow `~/.openclaw/.env` to retain override-only-blocked secrets as managed service keys, add a trusted exec inherited-env allowlist sourced from `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS`, and document the durable service env behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): everywhere-dangerous env vars such as `LD_PRELOAD`, `NODE_OPTIONS`, `BASH_ENV`, `DYLD_*`, and `GIT_DIR` stay blocked. `OPENCLAW_*` keys still do not gain trusted exec inheritance. Untrusted exec postures do not receive these secrets. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: `GH_TOKEN` placed in `~/.openclaw/.env` should survive managed gateway install/restart and be available to trusted gateway exec children, so `gh` can authenticate without manually injecting the token into each command. - Real environment tested: macOS OpenClaw gateway installed as a managed service, local OpenClaw checkout from v2026.5.2 with this patch applied, `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`, Telegram/agent exec path using `host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```sh cp ~/.env ~/.openclaw/.env chmod 600 ~/.openclaw/.env openclaw gateway stop openclaw gateway install --force openclaw gateway start openclaw gateway status ``` then ask the agent to run `gh auth status` and report back - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Telegram screenshot showing the live exec environment reporting `GH_TOKEN_present: true`, `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`, `gh auth status` logged in via `GH_TOKEN`, and `gh api user` returning the authenticated account JSON. Account details and token values are redacted. - Observed result after fix: trusted gateway exec can use `GH_TOKEN` inherited from the managed service env, and `gh` authenticates successfully from the OpenClaw exec environment. - What was not tested: Linux/systemd real-service behavior was not manually tested; the Linux path is covered by the shared service-env/unit tests. Non-trusted exec postures were verified by targeted unit tests, not by a second live service screenshot. The exec integration test (`bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts`) is skipped on Windows (shell-dependent); unit-level coverage runs on all platforms. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): - `before-1.jpg` and `before-2.jpg` — terminal screenshots showing the pre-fix managed-service behavior where the gateway env path stripped `GH_TOKEN` before it could reach the exec child, so `gh` could not authenticate after service install/restart. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `~/.openclaw/.env` parsing treated keys that are dangerous as agent request overrides the same as keys that are dangerous everywhere. This stripped operator-curated tool tokens like `GH_TOKEN` before the managed service could record them as durable env. Separately, exec env sanitization had no trusted-mode inherited-key allowlist, so there was no narrow path for managed service keys to reach trusted tool children. - Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered dangerous env stripping and service env formatting, but did not cover the desired operator-curated secret path from `~/.openclaw/.env` through managed service metadata into trusted gateway exec. - Contributing context (if known): the security model already distinguished inherited/env override risk, but the durable service env path did not preserve that distinction. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts` - `src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts` - `src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts` - `src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `GH_TOKEN` and similar override-only-blocked keys can be retained from owner-controlled durable service env, become managed service keys, and reach exec children only in the trusted gateway posture (`host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`). Unsafe keys and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: unit tests cover each policy seam, and the exec integration test proves the env-composition wiring reaches the child process under the relevant exec postures without requiring live GitHub credentials in CI. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A, new tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Operators can place `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env`, reinstall/start the managed gateway, and use `gh` from trusted gateway exec. This does not grant those tokens to untrusted exec postures. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser strips override-only-dangerous key -> managed gateway service has no GH_TOKEN-managed allowlist entry -> trusted exec child cannot authenticate gh After: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser keeps operator-curated override-only key -> install records GH_TOKEN in OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS -> trusted gateway exec allowlist permits inherited GH_TOKEN -> gh authenticates from the exec child ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this intentionally allows operator-curated service env secrets like `GH_TOKEN` to reach trusted gateway exec children. The path is constrained to keys recorded by the managed service env metadata and to the trusted exec posture. Everywhere-dangerous keys are still stripped, `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded, request overrides remain sanitized, and non-trusted postures do not inherit the allowlisted secret. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local managed OpenClaw gateway service - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw agent exec path, GitHub CLI (`gh`) as the real CLI consumer - Relevant config (redacted): `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`; `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` included `GH_TOKEN`; token value redacted in all evidence. ### Steps 1. Put `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env` and set owner-only permissions. 2. Reinstall/restart the managed gateway service with this patch applied. 3. Run a trusted gateway exec env probe and `gh auth status` / `gh api user`. 4. Run the targeted regression tests: ```sh pnpm test src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts ``` ### Expected - `process.env.GH_TOKEN` is present inside trusted gateway exec. - `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` contains `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` reports authentication via `GH_TOKEN`. - Untrusted exec postures do not receive the fixture token in tests. - Everywhere-dangerous and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. ### Actual - Live evidence showed `GH_TOKEN_present: true` and `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`. - `gh auth status` authenticated via `GH_TOKEN` and `gh api user` succeeded with redacted account output. - Targeted tests covered the parser, managed service allowlist, host env sanitizer, daemon install plan, and exec wiring. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Evidence files prepared for upload: - `before-1.jpg` - `before-2.jpg` - `after-1.jpg` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Managed gateway install/restart picks up `GH_TOKEN` from `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Trusted gateway exec sees `GH_TOKEN` and `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` includes `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` and `gh api user` work from the OpenClaw exec environment using `GH_TOKEN`. - Targeted tests exercise trusted, allowlist, and prompted exec postures plus dangerous-key exclusions. - Edge cases checked: - `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded from trusted inherited allowlist. - Everywhere-dangerous keys remain blocked even if listed. - PATH override smuggling remains blocked. - Default sanitizer behavior remains strict without `allowInheritedKeys`. - What you did **not** verify: - Manual Linux/systemd live service verification. - Every provider token named in the override-only dangerous list; `GH_TOKEN` is the live proof case and tests use representative fixture keys. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) Yes, for operators who want this behavior. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: place `GH_TOKEN` or similar operator-curated tool secret in `~/.openclaw/.env`, ensure the file is owner-only, then reinstall/restart the managed gateway service so `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` is regenerated. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: secrets that were previously stripped from managed service env can now reach trusted exec children. - Mitigation: only operator-curated durable service env keys are considered, only trusted gateway exec posture receives the inherited allowlist, and dangerous-everywhere plus `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Risk: operators may assume all exec postures receive `GH_TOKEN` after placing it in `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Mitigation: docs state that the exec layer decides inheritance by posture and that these keys are not auto-propagated to untrusted commands.
